title: Contains should be preferred instead of Any for simple equality checks
description: When testing if a collection contains a specific item by simple equality, both ICollection.Contains(T item) and IEnumerable.Any(x ⇒ x == item) can be used. However, Any searches the data structure in a linear manner using a foreach loop, whereas Contains is considerably faster in some collection types, because of the underlying implementation. More specifically: HashSet<T> is a hashtable, and therefore has an O(1) lookup SortedSet<T> is a red-black tree, and therefore has a O(logN) lookup List<T> is a linear search, and therefore has an O(N) lookup, but the EqualityComparer is optimized for the T type, which is not the case for Any For small collections, the performance difference may be negligible, but for large collections, it can be noticeable.
recommendation: Replace with the recommended alternative approach.

logic.detection_conditions: Detects .Any() calls with lambda expressions checking simple equality (x => x == value)
logic.triggering_conditions: Triggers when .Any() is called with a lambda that only checks equality (==) without additional conditions, suggesting .Contains() would be more efficient
logic.exclusion_conditions: Excludes .Any() with complex conditions (&&, ||, comparison operators in lambda), .Any() without arguments, logging statements, and test code
never_trigger_on: Any() with comparison operators in lambda expression
logic.reasoning: When checking if a collection contains a specific item by simple equality, Contains() is more efficient than Any() for certain collection types. HashSet<T> has O(1) Contains lookup vs O(N) Any. SortedSet<T> has O(logN) Contains vs O(N) Any. Even for List<T>, Contains uses optimized EqualityComparer. The rule detects .Any(x => x == value) patterns and suggests using .Contains(value) instead. Complex conditions with Any() are valid and excluded. Only targets CallExpression nodes to avoid false positives from generic type parameters.
